agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Integration of two previously independent bounded contexts (Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management) to enforce stock availability at order placement time and provide real-time stock visibility to the sales team. This is a fundamental shift from an eventually-consistent, decoupled model to a synchronous, coupled one.",
    "bounded_context": "Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B). The feature request threatens to collapse the boundary between these contexts, which exists for good reason.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "StockLevel", "definition": "Context A concept: the current physical inventory count for a Product in the warehouse. Integer >= 0." },
      { "term": "Product", "definition": "Exists in both contexts but means different things. In Context A, it is a warehouse-managed SKU with physical inventory. In Context B, it is a catalog item a customer can order." },
      { "term": "OrderLine", "definition": "Context B concept: a line item on a customer order, referencing a Product ID and a desired Quantity." },
      { "term": "Order", "definition": "Context B concept: a customer's intent to purchase one or more products. Currently placed without stock validation." },
      { "term": "Reservation", "definition": "NEW concept needed: a soft hold on stock allocated to a pending order, distinct from both StockLevel and picked inventory. Does not yet exist in either context." },
      { "term": "Available Stock", "definition": "NEW derived concept: StockLevel minus all active Reservations. This is what should drive the 'stock available' indicator — NOT raw StockLevel." },
      { "term": "Stock Availability Indicator", "definition": "A sales-facing view of whether sufficient stock exists to fulfill an order line. Must be understood as eventually consistent, not a guarantee." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "StockLevel in Context A must never go below zero.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a physical constraint — you cannot ship goods you do not have. Violating this means the warehouse data model is corrupt.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any Product, StockLevel >= 0 at all times, across all concurrent operations."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Context B must NOT directly read or write Context A's StockLevel. The bounded context boundary must be preserved.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If Sales directly queries or mutates warehouse state, we lose independent deployability, introduce tight coupling, and create a distributed data integrity nightmare. This is an architectural invariant, not a preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "Context B's codebase contains zero direct references to Context A's StockLevel entity, repository, or database tables."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "An Order that is 'blocked due to insufficient stock' is NOT rejected — it enters a held/pending state that can be resolved when stock arrives.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Business policy decision. Some businesses reject outright, others backorder. A VP could change this to 'reject' or 'backorder with ETA'. But the team must choose one — the spec says 'block', not 'reject'.",
      "testable_assertion": "When an order is placed with insufficient available stock, the order is persisted with a status indicating it is held for stock, not discarded."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Stock sufficiency checks must account for Reservations (stock already allocated to other pending orders), not just raw StockLevel.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without reservations, two orders placed simultaneously for the last unit would both pass the stock check and both be confirmed — violating BR-1 downstream when both are picked. This is a data consistency invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Available Stock = StockLevel - SUM(active Reservations). Stock sufficiency is evaluated against Available Stock, never raw StockLevel."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The 'stock available' indicator shown to the sales team is eventually consistent and must never be treated as a guarantee of fulfillment.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Between the moment the indicator is rendered and the moment the order is placed, stock can change. If the UI is treated as authoritative, the business will build processes on a lie. The indicator is informational only — the actual check happens at order placement time.",
      "testable_assertion": "The stock indicator is sourced from a read model (projection/cache), not a synchronous query to Context A. The UI labels the indicator as approximate or 'as of [timestamp]'."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Reservations must have a TTL (time-to-live) and expire if the order is not confirmed within a bounded time window.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Without expiration, abandoned or failed orders permanently reduce available stock — a slow inventory leak. The specific TTL duration is a business policy (could be 15 minutes or 24 hours), but the existence of expiration is critical to system health.",
      "testable_assertion": "A Reservation that is not confirmed within its TTL is automatically released, and Available Stock is recalculated."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Stock availability information flows from Context A to Context B via domain events or an anti-corruption layer, never via shared database.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Shared database coupling is the number one killer of bounded context independence. If both contexts read the same table, schema changes in A break B, deployment coupling is total, and transactional boundaries become impossible to reason about.",
      "testable_assertion": "Context B maintains its own local read model of stock availability, updated asynchronously from Context A events (e.g., StockLevelChanged, StockReserved, StockReleased)."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A customer places an order for 5 units of Product X, and Available Stock for Product X is 10",
      "when": "The order is submitted",
      "then": "The order is accepted, a Reservation of 5 units is created for Product X, and Available Stock drops to 5"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A customer places an order for 5 units of Product X, and Available Stock for Product X is 3",
      "when": "The order is submitted",
      "then": "The order is persisted with a 'held/insufficient stock' status, the customer is informed, and no Reservation is created (stock is not partially reserved unless the business explicitly wants partial fulfillment)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "Two customers simultaneously place orders for 8 units each of Product X, and Available Stock is 10",
      "when": "Both orders are submitted concurrently",
      "then": "Exactly one order is accepted and reserved; the other is held for insufficient stock. The system must never over-reserve."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A sales team member is viewing the order entry screen for Product X",
      "when": "Stock changes in Context A (items received or picked)",
      "then": "The stock availability indicator updates within an acceptable latency window (seconds, not minutes), showing the new approximate availability with a freshness timestamp"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "An order was accepted and a Reservation was created, but the order is subsequently cancelled or its TTL expires",
      "when": "The reservation is released",
      "then": "Available Stock increases by the reserved amount, and the released stock becomes available for other orders"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "Context A (Warehouse) is temporarily unavailable or its event stream is delayed",
      "when": "A customer places an order",
      "then": "The system degrades gracefully — either queues the order for later evaluation or accepts it provisionally, rather than hard-failing. The failure mode is a business decision that must be explicitly defined."
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Order with multiple OrderLines where some products have sufficient stock and others do not", "expected_behavior": "Business must decide: block the entire order (all-or-nothing) or allow partial fulfillment with held lines. This is NOT a technical decision — it changes the customer experience fundamentally." },
    { "scenario": "Stock received from supplier while an order is in 'held' state", "expected_behavior": "Held orders should be re-evaluated when stock arrives. Priority/ordering of held orders must be defined (FIFO by placement time is the obvious default, but the business may want priority customers served first)." },
    { "scenario": "Product exists in Context B's catalog but has never been stocked in Context A", "expected_behavior": "Available Stock should be treated as 0, not as 'unknown'. The stock indicator should show 'out of stock', not error." },
    { "scenario": "Rapid stock fluctuation (e.g., large supplier receipt followed immediately by a bulk pick)", "expected_behavior": "The eventually-consistent read model may briefly show stale data. The sales team must understand the indicator is approximate. The actual order-time check is authoritative." },
    { "scenario": "Reservation TTL expires on a legitimate order where customer is still completing payment", "expected_behavior": "The order should transition to held state and stock is released. The customer may need to re-confirm if stock was claimed by another order in the meantime." },
    { "scenario": "Context A publishes a StockLevelChanged event but Context B's event consumer is down", "expected_behavior": "Events must be durably stored (event log, message queue with persistence). When Context B recovers, it replays missed events. The read model may be stale during downtime but must eventually converge." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Blocking orders on stock reduces over-selling and the costly fallout of cancellations, refunds, and expedited shipping for backorders. However, false blocks (stale stock data causing unnecessary holds) directly reduce conversion and revenue. The reservation TTL creates a tension: too short loses legitimate sales, too long locks up stock artificially.",
    "operational": "This is a significant operational shift. The warehouse and sales teams, previously independent, now have a runtime dependency. Warehouse data quality becomes sales-critical — any stock count inaccuracy in Context A now directly blocks revenue. Warehouse receiving speed also matters: slow receipt processing means 'available' stock lags reality.",
    "user": "Sales team gets visibility they currently lack, which is valuable. But if the indicator is unreliable or laggy, trust erodes fast and they will route around it (e.g., calling the warehouse directly, ignoring the indicator). Customer experience improves if stock checks prevent false promises, but degrades if legitimate orders are incorrectly held due to stale data.",
    "risk": "The primary risk is introducing tight runtime coupling between two contexts that were designed to be independent. If Context A goes down, can Context B still accept orders? The answer to this question has existential implications for order throughput. Secondary risk: the feature request says 'real time' — this sets an expectation that is physically impossible with bounded contexts and will cause stakeholder friction if not managed.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Orders may be blocked or held that previously would have been accepted. Better long-term (fewer broken promises) but worse short-term if stock data is inaccurate." },
      { "group": "Sales Team", "impact": "Gains stock visibility (positive), but also gains a new failure mode: orders they expect to go through get blocked. Training required on what the indicator means and what 'held' status means." },
      { "group": "Warehouse Team", "impact": "Their stock accuracy now directly impacts sales. Receiving delays, miscounts, or slow data entry become revenue-blocking issues. This is a major increase in operational pressure." },
      { "group": "Engineering / Platform Team", "impact": "Must design and maintain the cross-context integration layer (events, read models, anti-corruption layer). This is the most architecturally significant change — new infrastructure, new failure modes, new monitoring requirements." },
      { "group": "Finance / Management", "impact": "Reduced over-selling is a clear win. But the reservation model introduces 'phantom stock locks' that can reduce apparent availability. Reporting must distinguish between physical stock and available stock." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The feature request is dangerously underspecified. It conflates two very different capabilities (synchronous order blocking and real-time UI indicators) into a single sentence, leaves critical business decisions undefined (partial fulfillment? failure mode? reservation duration?), and uses the phrase 'real time' without defining acceptable latency. The domain analysis is sound, but the spec quality is low — implementing against this spec without clarification will produce something that 'works' but isn't 'correct'.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "The bounded context boundary must be preserved — shared database is not acceptable",
      "A Reservation concept is required to prevent double-selling under concurrency",
      "The stock indicator must be eventually consistent, not synchronous",
      "StockLevel >= 0 is an inviolable physical constraint"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Should insufficient stock block the entire order or just affected lines? (all-or-nothing vs. partial fulfillment)",
      "What is the failure mode when Context A is unavailable? (reject orders, accept provisionally, queue?)",
      "What is the acceptable reservation TTL?",
      "What does 'real time' mean to the sales team? (sub-second? under 5 seconds? under 30 seconds?)",
      "Should held orders auto-resolve when stock arrives, or require manual intervention?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The business wants to preserve bounded context independence (not merge A and B)",
      "Orders are not rejected outright — 'block' means 'hold for resolution', not 'discard'",
      "Partial fulfillment is NOT desired (entire order blocked if any line insufficient) — but this assumption may be wrong",
      "The stock indicator is for internal sales team use, not customer-facing (the spec says 'sales team')"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Automatically block it if stock is insufficient' — does 'block' mean reject the order, hold it for later fulfillment, or something else? And does 'insufficient' mean insufficient for ALL lines or ANY line? This single sentence encodes at least two major business decisions that are not specified.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A) Reject the order entirely — customer must re-order when stock is available",
        "B) Hold the order in pending state — auto-release when stock arrives (FIFO)",
        "C) Hold with manual review — sales team decides whether to wait or cancel",
        "D) Accept order, backorder insufficient lines — partial shipment"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B (hold with auto-release) is the most common pattern in order management. But this MUST be confirmed with the sales team and product owner."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Real time' stock indicator is not defined. In a bounded context architecture, true real-time requires synchronous cross-context queries, which destroys context independence. Eventually-consistent (seconds of lag) is architecturally sound but may not meet stakeholder expectations.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Sales Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A) Eventually consistent read model updated via events (1-5 second lag typical)",
        "B) Synchronous API call to Context A on each page load (tight coupling, fragile)",
        "C) Polling/webhook hybrid with sub-second targets (complex, expensive)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A. Manage sales team expectations that the indicator is approximate. Label it 'as of [timestamp]' in the UI."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No failure mode defined for when Context A (Warehouse) is unavailable. This is a critical architectural and business decision: if the warehouse system is down, does the entire sales process stop?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales Team", "Management"],
      "options": [
        "A) Fail closed — no orders accepted when warehouse is unreachable (safe but revenue-blocking)",
        "B) Fail open — accept orders provisionally, check stock when warehouse recovers (risky but preserves revenue)",
        "C) Degrade gracefully — accept orders but flag them as 'unverified', process when possible"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C. Revenue preservation with transparency. But this must be a conscious business decision, not an engineering default."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Multi-line order with mixed stock availability is unspecified. If an order has 3 lines and only 1 is out of stock, what happens? This fundamentally changes the fulfillment model.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales Team", "Warehouse Team", "Finance"],
      "options": [
        "A) All-or-nothing: block entire order if any line is insufficient",
        "B) Partial fulfillment: ship what's available, hold the rest",
        "C) Customer choice: present the situation and let them decide"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Start with Option A (simplest, safest). Evolve to Option C if the business demands it. Option B has significant warehouse and invoicing complexity."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Any implementation that has Context B directly querying Context A's database or StockLevel entity", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2, BR-7 — Bounded context boundary violation. This creates deployment coupling, shared schema dependency, and makes both contexts fragile." },
    { "violation": "Any implementation that checks raw StockLevel without accounting for existing Reservations", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4 — Concurrent orders will over-sell, violating BR-1 downstream at pick time." },
    { "violation": "Any implementation that treats the sales UI stock indicator as an authoritative stock guarantee", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5 — The indicator is informational. The authoritative check happens at order placement, not at UI render time." },
    { "violation": "Any implementation that permanently locks stock on Reservations without a TTL or release mechanism", "business_rule_broken": "BR-6 — Abandoned orders will slowly drain available stock to zero, creating a phantom stockout." }
  ]
}
